q: What is the Microsoft Power Platform CLI (pac) primarily used for?
o: Command-line scaffolding and management of PCF, solutions and auth
Editing canvas app screens by drag-and-drop
why: pac CLI scaffolds PCF/plug-in projects and manages solutions, environments and authentication.

q: Which tooling integrates Power Platform development into Visual Studio / VS Code?
why: Power Platform Tools for Visual Studio/VS Code bring CLI and project support into the IDE.

q: Decide whether each developer-tooling statement is true.

rows.s: NuGet packages exist for building Dataverse plug-ins/assemblies.
There is a dedicated Connector CLI for custom connectors.
The CLI cannot authenticate to an environment.

why: NuGet supplies Dataverse SDK packages; a Connector CLI exists; pac auth manages environment sign-in.

q: Which command initializes and helps build a PCF component from the CLI?
why: pac pcf init scaffolds a code-component project; npm build then compiles it.

q: In the CLI exercise, what is the FIRST task before creating a component?
why: You install the Power Platform CLI before scaffolding or using components.

q: After building a PCF component, how do you get it into an environment during development?
o: Push it with the CLI (pac pcf push) into the Dataverse environment
Add it to the app title
why: pac pcf push deploys the component to the environment for testing during development.

q: Decide whether each CLI statement is true.

rows.s: npm build compiles the component's TypeScript/resources.
The CLI can create a solution project for packaging.

why: npm build compiles the component; pac solution init creates a solution project.

q: Which command family manages solutions from the CLI?
why: pac solution handles init, packing, exporting and importing solutions.

q: How do managed and unmanaged solutions differ in intent?
o: Unmanaged for development; managed for downstream (test/prod) deployment
Managed for development; unmanaged for production
why: You develop in unmanaged and deploy managed to target environments.

o: Multiple solutions stack, and the top layer determines the effective behavior
Each solution deletes the previous one
Only one solution can ever exist
Layers apply only to canvas apps
why: Solution layering stacks changes; the topmost active layer wins for a component.

q: Decide whether each solution statement is true.

rows.s: Solution dependencies must be present in the target to import successfully.
Managed solutions are typically used to deploy to production.
Unmanaged solutions are the recommended production artifact.

why: Dependencies must exist in the target; managed is the production artifact, unmanaged is for dev.

q: A component won't import because a required table it uses is missing in the target. What is the cause?
why: Missing prerequisites are unmet solution dependencies that block import.

q: Which tool converts a solution .zip into source-control-friendly files (and back)?
why: SolutionPackager / pac solution unpack decomposes a solution for source control; pack rebuilds it.

q: Why unpack a solution into individual files for source control?
o: To track and merge component changes and enable proper ALM
why: Unpacked files enable diffing, merging and versioning for healthy ALM.

q: Decide whether each packaging statement is true.

rows.s: A solution can be packaged as managed or unmanaged.
Packaging tools support automation in build pipelines.

why: You can pack managed/unmanaged and automate packaging in CI/CD (Build Tools).

q: Which combination best supports a professional developer workflow?
o: CLI + source control + SolutionPackager for repeatable ALM
why: CLI, source control and SolutionPackager together give repeatable, automatable ALM.
